title: Map Themes and Storylines
subtitle: A narrative mapping shape for when the manuscript exists enough that themes, arcs, and characters need their own structure.
scope: One manuscript where themes, storylines, and character carriers need to be tracked together.
sourceCoverage: Template only; replace placeholder themes, storylines, characters, scenes, and source material with real manuscript references.

templateMeta.useWhen: Use this when chapter progress alone is no longer enough and you need to see how themes and storylines are actually being carried through the manuscript.
templateMeta.operatingThesis: Treat the manuscript as meaning-carrying threads: themes, storylines, and character pressure should stay separate enough that thinness, overload, and imbalance become visible.
notFor: structural act planning without thematic analysis

objective: Make the manuscript's meaning threads explicit so a reader can see which themes, storylines, and characters are actually carrying the book.
successCondition: A reader can identify the major themes, how each storyline moves them, which characters carry the pressure, and where the manuscript is thin or overloaded.

callouts.title: How to use this template
items: Use this once the book has enough material that themes and storylines are hard to keep in your head.
Keep chapters out of this template unless they are serving a theme, a storyline turn, or a character pressure point.

callout.title: Template intent
items: This template is for structural meaning, not chapter count.
If you mainly need to know what to draft next, stay in the simpler book-writing starter.

data.name: Homecoming without belonging
data.articulation: Returning home intensifies estrangement rather than restoring comfort.
data.whyItMatters: This theme defines the emotional lens of the book and keeps the setting from becoming nostalgic wallpaper.
pressurePoints: The kitchen-table chapter currently carries more thematic weight than the opening, which may make the start feel underpowered.

data.name: Memory versus evidence
data.articulation: What people remember about the archive does not match what the remaining evidence suggests.
data.whyItMatters: This theme keeps the investigation from collapsing into a simple family-secret reveal.
pressurePoints: The confrontation scene hints at this thread, but the manuscript still needs one quieter scene where the contradiction is felt, not just explained.

data.name: Family return
data.currentTrajectory: Starts with displacement, tightens through domestic friction, and breaks open during the late confrontation.
beats: Kitchen-table scene surfaces the unspoken debt.
Confrontation turns private discomfort into open conflict.

data.name: Archive hunt
data.arcRole: Investigative line that keeps the emotional story moving toward discovery.
data.currentTrajectory: Strong in the middle, but the opening setup still needs one early sign that the archive matters.
beats: Archive room introduces the concrete absence.
Later confrontation reveals the removal was deliberate.

data.name: Lena
data.roleInStory: Primary witness and the person whose return reactivates every dormant tension.
data.currentPressure: She has to decide whether to protect family memory or follow the evidence wherever it leads.
continuityConcerns: Make sure her practical archive knowledge is seeded before the investigation turns technical.

data.name: Mother
data.roleInStory: Carrier of the family's silence and the social cost of the buried debt.
data.currentPressure: She needs to feel dangerous and understandable at the same time.
continuityConcerns: Her motives are strong in confrontation, but the quieter maternal texture needs one earlier scene.
